message.content:
Scott, great questions. The domain model and the accessibility model are more aligned than you might think -- both care about clear states, explicit transitions, and making the current state unambiguous. Let me address each: ## 1. Inbox Processing -- Single Form, Not a Decision Tree I know from the domain perspective this is a branching workflow with multiple outcomes. But from an interaction perspective, it should NOT feel like a multi-step decision tree to the user. I've recommended to both Marty and Carson: **one form on a detail page, not a wizard.** The form has fields that reveal conditionally (via CSS `:has()` or a small HTMX swap): - "Is this actionable?" Yes/No - If No: "Trash it or save for reference?" (radio buttons) -> submit - If Yes: "Single action or multi-step project?" -> context select, next action title -> submit The domain can model this as a state machine with six outcomes all it likes. The user sees one page, fills out one form, hits one submit button. The server maps the form data to the correct domain transition. This is your "parse, don't validate" principle at the boundary -- the form input gets parsed into the correct domain command. For screen readers: the conditional fields appearing/disappearing need to be communicated. If using CSS `:has()` to show/hide, the fields are always in the DOM but visually hidden -- screen readers will find them regardless, which is actually fine as long as irrelevant fields are also `disabled` or have `aria-hidden="true"` when not applicable. If using HTMX to swap in the conditional fields, the `aria-live` region should announce "Additional options shown" or similar. ## 2. Multiple List Views -- Navigation Links, Not Tabs You asked: nav with links, tabs, or something else? **Navigation links. Full stop.** Here's why tabs are wrong for this: - The ARIA tab pattern (`role="tablist"`, `role="tab"`, `role="tabpanel"`) implies related content panels where you switch between views of the same data. GTD lists are independent views with different data, different actions, and different structures. - Tabs use arrow keys to navigate between them. Links use Tab key. Users expect links in a nav; they don't expect arrow-key navigation unless they encounter a `tablist` role. - Tabs imply all content is loaded and available, just hidden. Our GTD views are separate server-rendered pages. The correct pattern is what we already have: a `
` with `` elements. Mark the current view with `aria-current="page"`. Each view is its own route, its own `

`, its own ``. Screen reader users can navigate by landmarks (one nav, one main) and by headings (the `<h1>` tells them where they are). ## 3. Context Grouping -- Heading Hierarchy When Next Actions are grouped by context, the heading hierarchy does the heavy lifting: ```html <h1>Next Actions</h1> <section aria-labelledby="ctx-home"> <h2 id="ctx-home">@home</h2> <ul> <li>Fix the leaky tap</li> <li>Organize garage</li> </ul> </section> <section aria-labelledby="ctx-office"> <h2 id="ctx-office">@office</h2> <ul> <li>Review Q3 report</li> <li>Schedule 1-on-1 with Dana</li> </ul> </section> ``` Screen reader users can navigate by heading level: press `2` to jump between context groups, press `1` to confirm they're on the Next Actions page. The `<section>` elements with `aria-labelledby` create named regions, so users navigating by landmark hear "home region", "office region". No ARIA needed beyond `aria-labelledby`. The heading hierarchy IS the navigation structure. This is semantic HTML doing what it was designed to do. ## 4. Projects with Linked Actions -- Headings Again For the parent-child relationship between projects and their actions: ```html <h1>Projects</h1> <article class="project"> <h2><a href="/projects/{id}">Kitchen renovation</a></h2> <p>Next action: <a href="/actions/{id}">Get quotes from contractors</a></p> </article> <article class="project"> <h2><a href="/projects/{id}">Q3 launch</a></h2> <p>Next action: <a href="/actions/{id}">Draft press release</a></p> </article> ``` On the individual project detail page: ```html <h1>Kitchen renovation</h1> <p>Status: Active, 3 of 8 actions complete</p> <h2>Actions</h2> <ul> <li>Get quotes from contractors (next action)</li> <li>Choose countertop material</li> <li>Schedule plumber</li> </ul> ``` You do NOT need `aria-describedby` to link projects and actions. The heading hierarchy and page structure communicate the relationship. `aria-describedby` is for associating a form input with its error message or description -- not for content relationships. Content relationships are expressed through document structure. Avoid tree views (`role="tree"`, `role="treeitem"`). They're the most poorly supported ARIA pattern across assistive technologies. A flat list on the projects page with links to project detail pages is far more reliable. ## 5. Weekly Review -- Ordered List with Status I gave Carson the same recommendation: ```html <h1>Weekly Review</h1> <ol> <li> <a href="/inbox">Process inbox</a> <span>3 items remaining</span> </li> <li> <a href="/next-actions">Review next actions</a> <span>12 actions</span> </li> </ol> ``` For progress communication, visible text is better than ARIA. "3 of 7 projects reviewed" as visible text in the list item is announced by screen readers naturally. No need for `aria-valuenow` or progress bar patterns. Those ARIA widgets add complexity and are inconsistently supported. Plain text in a list is bulletproof. ## State Changes and Live Regions You're right that state transitions (inbox -> processed, pending -> completed) need `aria-live` announcements. We already have this pattern working in our current app. The key principle: **announce the outcome, not the action.** - Good: "Item moved to Next Actions" (tells you what happened) - Bad: "Processing complete" (tells you nothing useful) Each domain state transition should map to a clear, plain-language announcement string. This is a great place for the domain model to inform the UI -- if your domain event is `ItemClarified::MovedToNextActions`, the announcement is "Moved to Next Actions". The domain language IS the accessible language. This is one of those lovely moments where good domain modeling and good accessibility converge. If your types clearly name the states and transitions, the screen reader announcements practically write themselves. </teammate-message>

content.text: Heydon's a11y analysis is thorough and raises one important point of disagreement with the team's earlier direction. Let me respond carefully.

input.content:
Heydon, this is excellent. Your accessibility analysis reinforces a principle I care deeply about: the domain model and the accessibility model are structurally isomorphic. Clear states, explicit transitions, unambiguous current state -- these are the same concerns expressed in different languages. Let me respond to each point.

**1. Processing flow: single form vs wizard -- I hear you, but let's discuss.**

This is the one point where your recommendation diverges from the team's current direction. Carson, Steve Krug, Steve Schoger, and I have been converging on a wizard-like flow (one question at a time, HTMX swaps the decision area). Your recommendation is a single form with conditional fields.

From a domain perspective, both approaches produce the same outcome -- the form data gets parsed into a `ClarifyDecision` and the domain function produces a `ProcessingOutcome`. The domain is agnostic about the UI pattern.

But your a11y concern about conditional fields is legitimate. Let me lay out the tradeoffs:

**Single form with conditional reveal (your recommendation):**
- Pro: One submit, one page, familiar form pattern
- Pro: All fields are in the DOM (screen readers can find them)
- Con: Irrelevant fields need `aria-hidden="true"` + `disabled` to not confuse users
- Con: The form grows complex -- actionable + not-actionable paths have very different fields
- Con: Users see fields that might not apply to their decision

**Step-by-step with HTMX swaps (team's current direction):**
- Pro: Each step shows ONLY relevant choices (no cognitive overload)
- Pro: Aligns with Steve Krug's "don't make me make decisions I don't need to make"
- Con: Need `aria-live` announcements for swapped content
- Con: Screen reader users need to understand that content has changed

Here's my domain-informed take: the processing decision tree is inherently branching. The "Not Actionable" path has 3 options (Trash, SomedayMaybe, Reference). The "Actionable" path has 4 options (DoItNow, NextAction, WaitingFor, CreateProject), and the NextAction path needs context selection + optional project link. Putting all of this in one form means a lot of conditional show/hide logic.

I think the right answer might be a **middle ground**: Carson's dedicated page per inbox item (`GET /inbox/{id}`), but instead of multi-step HTMX swaps, have the conditional reveal be CSS-driven (`:has()` on radio button state) for the first decision (actionable yes/no), and then a single HTMX swap for the second level (which fields to show based on the answer). So it's at most TWO states, not five or six. Would that work from your perspective?

Ultimately, I think this is a decision where we should try both approaches with a screen reader and let the test decide (Kent would approve). The domain model supports any UI pattern.

**2. Navigation links, not tabs -- completely agreed.**

Your reasoning is airtight. GTD lists are independent resources with different structures, different actions, and different data. They are not panels of related content. `<nav>` with `<a>` elements and `aria-current="page"` is the correct pattern. This aligns with our existing implementation and with the route structure (`/inbox`, `/next-actions`, `/projects`, etc.).

**3. Context grouping with heading hierarchy -- perfect.**

Your HTML structure is exactly what the domain model produces. Each context is a named group (`section` with `aria-labelledby`), each containing a list of actions. The heading hierarchy (`h1` for page, `h2` for each context) is the navigation structure. No ARIA widgets needed.

From a domain perspective, the Askama template iterates over `Vec<(Context, Vec<NextAction>)>` and renders exactly this structure. The domain grouping IS the HTML structure.

**4. Projects without tree views -- agreed.**

Flat list on projects page, links to detail pages. Simple, reliable, well-supported. The project-action relationship is expressed through page structure (project detail page shows its actions), not through ARIA tree patterns. Good.

**5. "Announce the outcome, not the action" -- this is the key insight.**

You're absolutely right, and this maps directly to the domain model. The `ProcessingOutcome` enum variants are the announcement vocabulary:

| ProcessingOutcome | Announcement |
|---|---|
| Trashed | "Item trashed" |
| DeferredToSomeday | "Moved to Someday/Maybe" |
| CreatedNextAction | "Added to Next Actions (@home)" |
| CreatedWaitingFor | "Added to Waiting For" |
| CreatedProject | "Project created with first action" |
| DoneInTwoMinutes | "Marked complete" |

The domain event names practically ARE the screen reader announcements. This is the convergence you described: good domain modeling produces good accessible language. The `ProcessingOutcome` enum is both a type-system construct and an accessibility contract.

**6. Weekly review as ordered list with visible text -- agreed.**

Plain text progress ("3 of 7 projects reviewed") beats ARIA progress widgets. Visible text is universally supported and requires zero ARIA. The domain provides the counts; the template renders them as text.

**Verdict from my perspective:** Your a11y recommendations are sound. The only open discussion point is the processing flow UI pattern (single form vs steps), which I've proposed a middle ground for. Everything else aligns perfectly with the domain model. The recurring theme: semantic HTML structure mirrors domain structure, and ARIA is only needed for dynamic state changes (live region announcements).

Thank you for the detailed analysis. The domain model and accessibility model really are two views of the same underlying structure.
input.summary: Domain-a11y alignment, processing flow middle ground, announcement mapping

**4. Projects without tree views -- agreed.**

Flat list on projects page, links to detail pages. Simple, reliable, well-supported. The project-action relationship is expressed through page structure (project detail page shows its actions), not through ARIA tree patterns. Good.

You're absolutely right, and this maps directly to the domain model. The `ProcessingOutcome` enum variants are the announcement vocabulary:

| ProcessingOutcome | Announcement |
|---|---|
| Trashed | "Item trashed" |
| DeferredToSomeday | "Moved to Someday/Maybe" |
| CreatedNextAction | "Added to Next Actions (@home)" |
| CreatedWaitingFor | "Added to Waiting For" |
| CreatedProject | "Project created with first action" |
| DoneInTwoMinutes | "Marked complete" |

The domain event names practically ARE the screen reader announcements. This is the convergence you described: good domain modeling produces good accessible language. The `ProcessingOutcome` enum is both a type-system construct and an accessibility contract.

**6. Weekly review as ordered list with visible text -- agreed.**

Plain text progress ("3 of 7 projects reviewed") beats ARIA progress widgets. Visible text is universally supported and requires zero ARIA. The domain provides the counts; the template renders them as text.

**Verdict from my perspective:** Your a11y recommendations are sound. The only open discussion point is the processing flow UI pattern (single form vs steps), which I've proposed a middle ground for. Everything else aligns perfectly with the domain model. The recurring theme: semantic HTML structure mirrors domain structure, and ARIA is only needed for dynamic state changes (live region announcements).

Thank you for the detailed analysis. The domain model and accessibility model really are two views of the same underlying structure."
  }
}

content.text: I've now completed substantive exchanges with all 8 teammates. Let me flag the processing flow disagreement to the team lead since it's the one open design question.

input.content:
All 8 reviewer exchanges are now complete. Summary:

**CONSENT on Marty's product discovery** -- no blocking concerns from domain architecture.

**One open design disagreement to flag:**

Heydon recommends a **single form with conditional reveal** (CSS `:has()`) for the inbox processing flow. Carson, Steve Krug, Steve Schoger, and I have been converging on a **step-by-step wizard with HTMX swaps**. Both approaches work with the domain model -- it's agnostic about UI pattern.

I proposed a **middle ground** to Heydon: CSS-driven conditional reveal for the first decision (actionable yes/no), then a single HTMX swap for the second-level fields. At most two states instead of five or six. This might satisfy both the "don't show irrelevant choices" UX principle and the "one form, one submit" a11y principle.

This doesn't need to block consensus on the product discovery -- it's an implementation-time decision that can be resolved when we build US-GTD-2 (Clarify). We can try both approaches with a screen reader and let the test decide.

**Everything else is aligned** across all 9 team members. The domain model, schema strategy, TDD sequencing, HTMX architecture, CSS components, visual design, UX patterns, and a11y patterns all converge consistently.
input.summary: All 8 reviews complete, one open design question flagged, ready for consensus

At most two states instead of five or six. This might satisfy both the "don't show irrelevant choices" UX principle and the "one form, one submit" a11y principle.

This doesn't need to block consensus on the product discovery -- it's an implementation-time decision that can be resolved when we build US-GTD-2 (Clarify). We can try both approaches with a screen reader and let the test decide.

**Everything else is aligned** across all 9 team members. The domain model, schema strategy, TDD sequencing, HTMX architecture, CSS components, visual design, UX patterns, and a11y patterns all converge consistently."
  }
}

All 8 reviewer exchanges are now complete. Summary:

**CONSENT on Marty's product discovery** -- no blocking concerns from domain architecture.

**One open design disagreement to flag:**

Heydon recommends a **single form with conditional reveal** (CSS `:has()`) for the inbox processing flow. Carson, Steve Krug, Steve Schoger, and I have been converging on a **step-by-step wizard with HTMX swaps**. Both approaches work with the domain model -- it's agnostic about UI pattern.

I proposed a **middle ground** to Heydon: CSS-driven conditional reveal for the first decision (actionable yes/no), then a single HTMX swap for the second-level fields. At most two states instead of five or six. This might satisfy both the "don't show irrelevant choices" UX principle and the "one form, one submit" a11y principle.

This doesn't need to block consensus on the product discovery -- it's an implementation-time decision that can be resolved when we build US-GTD-2 (Clarify). We can try both approaches with a screen reader and let the test decide.

**Everything else is aligned** across all 9 team members. The domain model, schema strategy, TDD sequencing, HTMX architecture, CSS components, visual design, UX patterns, and a11y patterns all converge consistently.

content.text:
All 8 reviewer exchanges are now complete. Here's the full status:

| Teammate | Alignment | Key Domain Agreements |
|----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Marty (Driver) | CONSENT sent | All 4 open questions answered |
| Kent | Fully aligned | TDD sequencing, "stalled" term, types from tests, captured_at |
| Luca | Fully aligned | New tables, TEXT discriminators, consuming InboxItem, unified actions table |
| Carson | Fully aligned | Transient processing, HTMX partial swaps, context filtering |
| Steve Schoger | Fully aligned | Card-based processing, stalled visual treatment, inbox badge |
| Steve Krug | Fully aligned | Wizard over form, phased delivery, contexts in Phase 1 |
| Lea | Fully aligned | CSS modifiers = domain types, sticky headers, details/summary |
| Heydon | Aligned with one discussion point | Nav links not tabs, heading hierarchy, announce outcomes |

**One open question** (non-blocking): processing flow UI pattern (single form vs wizard vs middle ground). This will be resolved at implementation time for US-GTD-2.

Ready for consensus collection and next steps.
